title: Cardiac Arrest — Adult ALS
oneLiner: Sudden loss of cardiac output requiring immediate CPR, defibrillation, and ALS algorithm; survival depends on early recognition and action.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Reversible cause – tension pneumothorax:** Tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds on one side, distended neck veins, shock. Needle decompression before any drugs.
- **Reversible cause – airway obstruction:** No air movement despite head-tilt/chin-lift; gurgling, stridor, or obvious foreign body. Immediate basic airway manoeuvres or suction.
- **Reversible cause – hypovolaemia (exsanguination):** No pulse, but chest is empty on ultrasound (no cardiac tamponade, no pneumothorax) and rapid exsanguination history. Massive transfusion protocol, not just ALS drugs.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Collapse, unresponsive, not breathing normally (agonal gasps may be present)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No pulse (carotid/central)
  - Nociceptive stimulus: no response
  - Agonal respirations (slow, irregular, gasping) – **treat as cardiac arrest**
  - Pupils: dilated and fixed (late sign)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypoxic arrest** (e.g. choking, drowning, asthma) – often preceded by respiratory distress, monitor for airway obstruction
- **Cardiac arrest due to arrhythmia** (VF/pVT, asystole, PEA) – ECG during CPR
- **Non-cardiac causes:**
  - Tension pneumothorax – absent breath sounds, hyperresonance
  - Tamponade – pulsus paradoxus (if any output), Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled hearts, JVP)
  - Pulmonary embolism – signs of DVT, recent surgery, sudden onset
- **Splitter:** The rhythm on monitor or pads – if shockable (VF/pVT) → defibrillate; if non-shockable (asystole/PEA) → treat reversible causes
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Defibrillator pads attached** – analyse rhythm immediately
  - **CPR** with rate 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm
  - **End-tidal CO₂** (capnography) – waveform confirms airway; <10 mmHg after 20 min suggests poor prognosis
  - **Ultrasound (Focused Echo)** – if trained: look for pericardial effusion, left ventricular movement, pulmonary embolism signs
- **Bloods:**
  - **Arterial blood gas** (post-ROSC or during arrest if available) – pH, lactate, K+, glucose, CO₂
  - **Full blood count, U&E, troponin, clotting** – once ROSC achieved
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** (post-ROSC) – confirm tube position, exclude pneumothorax
  - **CT head** if ROSC and suspicion of intracranial cause (e.g. subarachnoid)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **4Hs & 4Ts** – Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypothermia, Hyper/hypokalaemia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Thrombosis (MI/PE), Toxins
  - **Modified ROSC criteria:** Any return of spontaneous circulation with palpable pulse
- **Never-miss test:** **Verify rhythm & check for reversible causes** – failure to identify a shockable rhythm or treat a 4H/4T leads to ongoing arrest
sections.Management:
#### Immediate (first hour / ED):
- **Start high-quality CPR** – rate 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm, allow chest recoil.
- **Attach defibrillator** – analyse rhythm every 2 min.
- **Shockable rhythm (VF/pVT):**
  - Immediate defibrillation: **120-200 J biphasic** (or device setting)
  - CPR for 2 min → reanalyse
  - After 3rd shock: give **adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO** (immediately, not before shock)
  - After 3rd shock: give **amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (if refractory)
  - Continue adrenaline 1 mg every 3-5 min
- **Non-shockable rhythm (asystole/PEA):**
  - CPR for 2 min → reanalyse
  - **Adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO** as soon as IV/IO access gained, then every 3-5 min
- **Airway management:**
  - Basic: bag-valve-mask with O₂ 15 L/min
  - Advanced: supraglottic airway (i-gel) or tracheal intubation if skilled; capnography mandatory
- **Treat reversible causes** (4Hs & 4Ts) throughout – e.g. needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, IV fluids for hypovolaemia, calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia

#### Ward (first 24-72h) – post-ROSC:
- **Targeted temperature management:** Cool to 32-36°C for 24h if comatose (use cooling device or cold IV fluids per local protocol)
- **Coronary angiography** (any ST elevation or high suspicion of ACS) – discuss with cardiology
- **CT head** if ROSC and suspicion of intracranial pathology
- **Control seizures** – levetiracetam or phenytoin (avoid prophylaxis)
- **Ventilation:** Aim PaO₂ 10-13 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5-6.0 kPa, avoid hyperoxia/hypercapnia
- **Lab monitoring:** Serial troponin, blood gases, lactate, U&E, glucose

#### Long-term / outpatient:
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** if indicated (e.g. VF arrest, structural heart disease)
- **Cardiology follow-up** for secondary prevention (medication, lifestyle)
- **Neuroprognostication** after 72h–7 days post-ROSC (if no awakening): clinical exam, EEG, somatosensory evoked potentials

#### Default Orders (if admitted):
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for haemodynamics)
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg sc daily (if no bleeding risk)
- **Diet/NBM:** NBM initially; review after extubation
- **Observation frequency:** Repeat vital signs (NEWS2) every 15 min for 1h, then hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly; continuous ECG monitoring
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Adrenaline (epinephrine) 1 mg IV/IO** every 3-5 min during arrest – use undiluted (1:1000) 1 mg in 1 mL prefilled syringe. No dose adjustment.
- **Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (after 3 shocks for VF/pVT) – give over 1-2 min; may repeat with **150 mg** if refractory.
- **Amiodarone alternative:** Lidocaine 1 mg/kg IV/IO (if amiodarone unavailable)
- **Atropine** – no longer recommended for pulseless arrest (except in specific bradyarrhythmias with pulses)
- **Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV** – only if severe acidosis (pH <7.1), tricyclic overdose, or hyperkalaemia – check local policy
- **Calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% IV** – for hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker overdose
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Amiodarone – avoid if pre-existing bradycardia or long QT (but in arrest, risk-benefit favours use)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for arrest drugs; adjust maintenance doses post-ROSC
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Adrenaline overdose** (e.g. 1:1000 given IV instead of 1:10,000) – causes severe hypertension, VF. Always use prefilled 1 mg ampoule.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) instability** – hypotension, arrhythmias, hypoxic brain injury
  - **Post-cardiac arrest syndrome** – systemic inflammatory response, coagulopathy, multi-organ failure
  - **Rib fractures, pneumothorax, pericardial effusion** from CPR
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** – especially if airway not protected
- **Chronic:**
  - **Hypoxic brain injury** – cognitive deficits, vegetative state, up to 50% survivors
  - **Cardiomyopathy** (if underlying ischaemic or tachyarrhythmic cause)
  - **Psychological sequelae** (PTSD, depression)
sections.Prognosis: - **In-hospital cardiac arrest survival to discharge:** ~15-25%; **out-of-hospital:** ~8-12%. Best in shockable rhythms (VF/pVT) with early defibrillation. Poor if asystole or prolonged CPR (>20 min without ROSC).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do start CPR immediately** – every minute delay reduces survival by 10%.
- **Check defibrillator pads are stuck firmly** – poor contact causes shock failure.
- **Use capnography throughout** – a sudden rise in ETCO₂ suggests ROSC; a sustained low value (<10 mmHg) after 20 min indicates futility.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT stop CPR for rhythm analysis** – wait until end of 2-minute cycle (unless shock is due).
- **Do NOT give adrenaline before defibrillation** in shockable rhythms – give immediately after the 3rd shock (not before).
- **Do NOT forget reversible causes** – always mentally run through 4Hs & 4Ts between cycles.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Any new chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or difficulty breathing.
- **Return if:** Symptoms of neurological deterioration (confusion, weakness, seizures).
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; consider pacemaker/ICD clinic.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call cardiac arrest team:** Any unresponsive patient not breathing normally – dial 2222 (UK).
- **Call senior/ICU:** If ROSC achieved but patient is unstable (SBP <90 mmHg, GCS <9, hypotension despite fluids, wide-complex tachyarrhythmia).
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single parameter scoring 3 (e.g. RR ≤8 or ≥25, SpO₂ ≤91%, HR ≤40 or ≥130, SBP ≤90)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (arterial)
  - GCS ≤8 (or deterioration)
  - PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio <200 (indicates severe lung injury)

rawMarkdown:
## Cardiac Arrest — Adult ALS

**One-liner:** Sudden loss of cardiac output requiring immediate CPR, defibrillation, and ALS algorithm; survival depends on early recognition and action.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Reversible cause – tension pneumothorax:** Tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds on one side, distended neck veins, shock. Needle decompression before any drugs.
- **Reversible cause – airway obstruction:** No air movement despite head-tilt/chin-lift; gurgling, stridor, or obvious foreign body. Immediate basic airway manoeuvres or suction.
- **Reversible cause – hypovolaemia (exsanguination):** No pulse, but chest is empty on ultrasound (no cardiac tamponade, no pneumothorax) and rapid exsanguination history. Massive transfusion protocol, not just ALS drugs.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Collapse, unresponsive, not breathing normally (agonal gasps may be present)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No pulse (carotid/central)
  - Nociceptive stimulus: no response
  - Agonal respirations (slow, irregular, gasping) – **treat as cardiac arrest**
  - Pupils: dilated and fixed (late sign)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypoxic arrest** (e.g. choking, drowning, asthma) – often preceded by respiratory distress, monitor for airway obstruction
- **Cardiac arrest due to arrhythmia** (VF/pVT, asystole, PEA) – ECG during CPR
- **Non-cardiac causes:**
  - Tension pneumothorax – absent breath sounds, hyperresonance
  - Tamponade – pulsus paradoxus (if any output), Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled hearts, JVP)
  - Pulmonary embolism – signs of DVT, recent surgery, sudden onset
- **Splitter:** The rhythm on monitor or pads – if shockable (VF/pVT) → defibrillate; if non-shockable (asystole/PEA) → treat reversible causes

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Defibrillator pads attached** – analyse rhythm immediately
  - **CPR** with rate 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm
  - **End-tidal CO₂** (capnography) – waveform confirms airway; <10 mmHg after 20 min suggests poor prognosis
  - **Ultrasound (Focused Echo)** – if trained: look for pericardial effusion, left ventricular movement, pulmonary embolism signs
- **Bloods:**
  - **Arterial blood gas** (post-ROSC or during arrest if available) – pH, lactate, K+, glucose, CO₂
  - **Full blood count, U&E, troponin, clotting** – once ROSC achieved
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** (post-ROSC) – confirm tube position, exclude pneumothorax
  - **CT head** if ROSC and suspicion of intracranial cause (e.g. subarachnoid)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **4Hs & 4Ts** – Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypothermia, Hyper/hypokalaemia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Thrombosis (MI/PE), Toxins
  - **Modified ROSC criteria:** Any return of spontaneous circulation with palpable pulse
- **Never-miss test:** **Verify rhythm & check for reversible causes** – failure to identify a shockable rhythm or treat a 4H/4T leads to ongoing arrest

### Management

#### Immediate (first hour / ED):
- **Start high-quality CPR** – rate 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm, allow chest recoil.
- **Attach defibrillator** – analyse rhythm every 2 min.
- **Shockable rhythm (VF/pVT):**
  - Immediate defibrillation: **120-200 J biphasic** (or device setting)
  - CPR for 2 min → reanalyse
  - After 3rd shock: give **adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO** (immediately, not before shock)
  - After 3rd shock: give **amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (if refractory)
  - Continue adrenaline 1 mg every 3-5 min
- **Non-shockable rhythm (asystole/PEA):**
  - CPR for 2 min → reanalyse
  - **Adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO** as soon as IV/IO access gained, then every 3-5 min
- **Airway management:**
  - Basic: bag-valve-mask with O₂ 15 L/min
  - Advanced: supraglottic airway (i-gel) or tracheal intubation if skilled; capnography mandatory
- **Treat reversible causes** (4Hs & 4Ts) throughout – e.g. needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, IV fluids for hypovolaemia, calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia

#### Ward (first 24-72h) – post-ROSC:
- **Targeted temperature management:** Cool to 32-36°C for 24h if comatose (use cooling device or cold IV fluids per local protocol)
- **Coronary angiography** (any ST elevation or high suspicion of ACS) – discuss with cardiology
- **CT head** if ROSC and suspicion of intracranial pathology
- **Control seizures** – levetiracetam or phenytoin (avoid prophylaxis)
- **Ventilation:** Aim PaO₂ 10-13 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5-6.0 kPa, avoid hyperoxia/hypercapnia
- **Lab monitoring:** Serial troponin, blood gases, lactate, U&E, glucose

#### Long-term / outpatient:
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** if indicated (e.g. VF arrest, structural heart disease)
- **Cardiology follow-up** for secondary prevention (medication, lifestyle)
- **Neuroprognostication** after 72h–7 days post-ROSC (if no awakening): clinical exam, EEG, somatosensory evoked potentials

#### Default Orders (if admitted):
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for haemodynamics)
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg sc daily (if no bleeding risk)
- **Diet/NBM:** NBM initially; review after extubation
- **Observation frequency:** Repeat vital signs (NEWS2) every 15 min for 1h, then hourly for 4h, then 4-hourly; continuous ECG monitoring

### Drugs & Doses
- **Adrenaline (epinephrine) 1 mg IV/IO** every 3-5 min during arrest – use undiluted (1:1000) 1 mg in 1 mL prefilled syringe. No dose adjustment.
- **Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (after 3 shocks for VF/pVT) – give over 1-2 min; may repeat with **150 mg** if refractory.
- **Amiodarone alternative:** Lidocaine 1 mg/kg IV/IO (if amiodarone unavailable)
- **Atropine** – no longer recommended for pulseless arrest (except in specific bradyarrhythmias with pulses)
- **Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV** – only if severe acidosis (pH <7.1), tricyclic overdose, or hyperkalaemia – check local policy
- **Calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% IV** – for hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker overdose
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Amiodarone – avoid if pre-existing bradycardia or long QT (but in arrest, risk-benefit favours use)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for arrest drugs; adjust maintenance doses post-ROSC
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Adrenaline overdose** (e.g. 1:1000 given IV instead of 1:10,000) – causes severe hypertension, VF. Always use prefilled 1 mg ampoule.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - **Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) instability** – hypotension, arrhythmias, hypoxic brain injury
  - **Post-cardiac arrest syndrome** – systemic inflammatory response, coagulopathy, multi-organ failure
  - **Rib fractures, pneumothorax, pericardial effusion** from CPR
  - **Aspiration pneumonia** – especially if airway not protected
- **Chronic:**
  - **Hypoxic brain injury** – cognitive deficits, vegetative state, up to 50% survivors
  - **Cardiomyopathy** (if underlying ischaemic or tachyarrhythmic cause)
  - **Psychological sequelae** (PTSD, depression)

### Prognosis
- **In-hospital cardiac arrest survival to discharge:** ~15-25%; **out-of-hospital:** ~8-12%. Best in shockable rhythms (VF/pVT) with early defibrillation. Poor if asystole or prolonged CPR (>20 min without ROSC).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do start CPR immediately** – every minute delay reduces survival by 10%.
- **Check defibrillator pads are stuck firmly** – poor contact causes shock failure.
- **Use capnography throughout** – a sudden rise in ETCO₂ suggests ROSC; a sustained low value (<10 mmHg) after 20 min indicates futility.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT stop CPR for rhythm analysis** – wait until end of 2-minute cycle (unless shock is due).
- **Do NOT give adrenaline before defibrillation** in shockable rhythms – give immediately after the 3rd shock (not before).
- **Do NOT forget reversible causes** – always mentally run through 4Hs & 4Ts between cycles.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Any new chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or difficulty breathing.
- **Return if:** Symptoms of neurological deterioration (confusion, weakness, seizures).
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; consider pacemaker/ICD clinic.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call cardiac arrest team:** Any unresponsive patient not breathing normally – dial 2222 (UK).
- **Call senior/ICU:** If ROSC achieved but patient is unstable (SBP <90 mmHg, GCS <9, hypotension despite fluids, wide-complex tachyarrhythmia).
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single parameter scoring 3 (e.g. RR ≤8 or ≥25, SpO₂ ≤91%, HR ≤40 or ≥130, SBP ≤90)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (arterial)
  - GCS ≤8 (or deterioration)
  - PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio <200 (indicates severe lung injury)
